target.title: fix(acp): fail fast on dead spawns and recover stale disconnects
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: ACP sessions could report a successful `/acp spawn` even when the newly initialized runtime handle was already unusable, and stale gateway disconnects could leave cancel/close/early-turn recovery stuck on a bad cached handle.
- Why it matters: Operators can see contradictory UX (`Spawned` followed by immediate `ACP_TURN_FAILED`) and then lose reliable recovery controls for the affected ACP session.
- What changed: Probe the freshly initialized runtime status before reporting spawn success; clean up failed post-spawn sessions; treat explicit stale gateway disconnect close codes as recoverable for close, cancel, active-turn cancel, and early-turn retry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No ACP protocol/schema changes, no new public commands, no auth/provider changes, no gateway restart policy changes, and no broad retry policy for non-stale gateway errors.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes # (none)
- Related #63656
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Spawn success was emitted after `initializeSession` without verifying the new runtime handle could answer an immediate status check, and the ACP manager only treated `acpx exited ...` as a stale runtime-handle recovery signal.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no spawn-time liveliness guard, no regression for gateway-disconnect stale-handle recovery, and no active-turn cancel test for stale disconnect cleanup.
- Contributing context (if known): ACP transport disconnects surface as `Gateway disconnected: 1006` or `gateway closed (...)`; some close codes indicate stale transport, while others such as `1008` represent real authorization/pairing failures.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/acp spawn` fails and cleans up when the immediate status probe reports `Gateway disconnected: 1006`.
  - Spawn health checks call direct runtime status and do not depend on capability lookup.
  - Close, passive cancel, active-turn cancel, and early-turn retry recover known stale gateway disconnect close codes.
  - Non-stale gateway closures such as `1008 pairing required` are surfaced instead of swallowed.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior is localized to ACP command lifecycle and `AcpSessionManager` runtime-handle recovery, so mocked runtime tests can deterministically exercise every branch without a live ACP backend.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing ACP manager tests covered equivalent `acpx exited ...` recovery and normal active-turn cancel; this PR extends that pattern to gateway disconnect errors and post-spawn health checks.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `/acp spawn` no longer reports success when the freshly initialized ACP runtime immediately fails status with a gateway disconnect; it returns the ACP error and cleans up the failed session.
- `/acp cancel` and `/acp close` can recover stale gateway-disconnected runtime handles for explicit stale/disconnect close codes.
- First ACP turns that fail immediately with a stale gateway disconnect get one fresh-handle retry.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
/acp spawn -> initializeSession OK -> "Spawned" -> first prompt -> ACP_TURN_FAILED
           -> cancel/close can stay stuck on stale cached handle

After:
/acp spawn -> initializeSession OK -> direct runtime status probe
            -> fail? cleanup + ACP error
            -> pass? bind/report spawn success

stale gateway disconnect during close/cancel/early first turn
  -> clear cached handle -> recover or retry once
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node.js via repo `pnpm` lanes
- Model/provider: ACP runtime mocked in regression tests
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP slash command lifecycle
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP enabled with `acpx` backend

### Steps

1. Spawn an ACP session while the freshly initialized runtime status path fails with `Gateway disconnected: 1006`.
2. Attempt cancel/close against an ACP session whose cached runtime handle is stale from a gateway disconnect.
3. Attempt an early first turn that fails before output with `Gateway disconnected: 1006`.

### Expected

- Spawn fails immediately with cleanup instead of reporting false success.
- Recoverable stale disconnects clear the cached handle and allow recovery/fresh-handle retry.
- Non-stale gateway errors remain visible.

### Actual

- Before this fix, spawn could report success before immediate turn failure, and stale gateway disconnects were not covered by the existing stale-runtime recovery handling.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config vitest.config.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
  - `3` files passed, `166` tests passed.
- `pnpm check:changed`
  - Stopped during the final broad e2e shard because the local changed-gate plan selected 293 targets. Completed before stop: core typecheck, core test typecheck, lint, import-cycle checks, gateway shard (`91` files / `990` tests), auto-reply shard (`38` files / `494` tests), agents shard (`41` files / `746` tests), and the smaller ACP/plugin/CLI/commands shards shown in local output.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Spawn-health failure returns the ACP error, avoids binding, closes the runtime, and deletes the failed session.
  - Spawn-health success path does not call capability lookup.
  - Stale gateway disconnect recovery clears cached handles for close, passive cancel, active-turn cancel, and early-turn retry.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Recoverable gateway disconnects are limited to explicit stale/disconnect close codes.
  - `1008 pairing required` is not treated as recoverable.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live ACP backend replay in this PR cycle.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: A post-spawn status probe can reject a session that is already unstable immediately after initialization.
  - Mitigation: This is intentional fail-fast behavior to avoid false-success spawns; failed sessions are cleaned up.
- Risk: Gateway disconnect recovery could hide real gateway failures if classified too broadly.
  - Mitigation: Recovery is limited to an explicit stale/disconnect close-code allowlist, with negative tests for `1008 pairing required`.

target.title: fix(signal): preserve uuid allowlist entries for command auth
target.body:
This fixes a Signal command-authorization regression for paired senders stored as `uuid:...`.

Signal text commands such as `/help` and `/status` already existed, and UUID-based Signal sender support already existed. The regression was introduced in the shared Signal config/security adapter, which normalized all `allowFrom` entries as E.164 phone numbers. That broke command auth and DM-policy normalization for UUID-based paired senders.

This change adds Signal-specific allowlist normalization that:

- preserves `uuid:` sender ids
- still normalizes phone numbers as E.164
- uses the same normalization for both shared config formatting and DM-policy normalization

Validation:

- `vitest run extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts`
- passed with `33` tests

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test

  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient

- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

### Actual

- `tsgo:core` passes.

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.

  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.

target.title: feat(gateway): add queue diagnostics snapshot
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: operators and contributors have no first-class way to inspect live lane backlog, active work, and tracked stuck sessions when debugging queue stalls.
- Why it matters: queue/lane issues currently require log scraping, which makes stuck-session and lane-backlog triage slow and hard to automate.
- What changed: added a shared queue diagnostics snapshot, a read-only `diagnostics.queue` Gateway RPC, and `openclaw gateway diagnostics queue [--json|--all]` CLI output plus docs/tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add an in-agent `queue_status` tool, does not change scheduling/dispatch behavior, and does not persist queue state.

AI-assisted: Yes (Codex)
Testing degree: fully tested for the touched paths

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #9797

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New Gateway RPC method: `diagnostics.queue`
- New CLI command: `openclaw gateway diagnostics queue`
- `--json` returns the raw snapshot for scripting
- `--all` includes idle lanes and tracked idle session states

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

Adds one new read-only operator RPC for live queue/session diagnostics. Risk is limited to exposing in-memory operational metadata to already authorized operator clients. Mitigation: method is scoped to `operator.read`, returns no secrets, and does not mutate runtime state.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm/corepack
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local dev config

### Steps

1. Enqueue work into a session lane so one task is active and another is queued.
2. Call `openclaw gateway diagnostics queue --json` or invoke `diagnostics.queue` over Gateway RPC.
3. Confirm the response shows active/queued counts, lane ages, and tracked session state.

### Expected

- Operators can see live queue/lane backlog and stuck-session candidates without parsing logs.

### Actual

- CLI and RPC now return a live snapshot built from in-memory queue state plus tracked diagnostic session state.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted Vitest for queue snapshotting, RPC handler, CLI wiring, and scope authorization; targeted `oxlint`; targeted `markdownlint`; full `pnpm build`.
- Edge cases checked: missing RPC params default to `includeIdle=false`; idle lanes/sessions omitted by default; stuck sessions are flagged when age exceeds the configured threshold.
- What you did **not** verify: live Gateway output against a running service with real channel traffic.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR/commit; stop using `diagnostics.queue` / `gateway diagnostics queue`.
- Files/config to restore: Gateway server method registration, CLI registration, and queue diagnostics helper files from `main`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: RPC call fails when `params` is omitted; queue snapshot output diverges from live backlog counts.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: session-level diagnostics depend on tracked session state and may be less complete than raw lane counts when a code path does not update diagnostic session state.
  - Mitigation: snapshot always reports raw lane counts independently, docs frame the session section as tracked runtime state, and the RPC remains read-only so follow-up improvements can extend coverage safely.

target.title: fix(reply): reset sessions after all-model timeout failures
target.body:
## Problem
When every model in the fallback chain times out, `runReplyAgent` currently leaves the conversation on the same session and returns a generic failure. The next user message then retries the same bloated or stuck session and can keep looping indefinitely.

Fixes #20910.

## What this changes
- detect timeout failures in the embedded fallback runner
- reset the session before replying so the next retry starts from a fresh transcript
- return a user-facing retry message after the reset succeeds
- add focused coverage for the timeout-reset path

## Why here
`runReplyAgent` already auto-recovers from compaction failures and role-order corruption by rotating the session. Timeout-driven session death spirals are the same class of failure: retrying on the same session is usually the wrong thing to do.

## Testing
- pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.test.ts

target.title: fix(cron): support isolated cron jobs in reminder guard + preserve counter across compaction
target.body:
## Problem

Two bugs in the unscheduled-reminder guard (#43292):

1. `hasSessionRelatedCronJobs` only matched session-bound cron jobs (by `sessionKey`). Isolated cron jobs — which have no `sessionKey`, only an `agentId` — were never matched, causing false-positive "no reminder scheduled" notes.

2. `resetForCompactionRetry` zeroed `successfulCronAdds`, so after a mid-turn compaction retry the guard fired even though a cron job was successfully created earlier in the same turn.

## Fix

1. Also match isolated jobs by `agentId` when no `sessionKey` is present on the job.
2. Preserve `successfulCronAdds` across compaction retries.

## Validation

7 new tests covering session-bound, isolated, disabled, empty, and error cases — all passing. Lint clean.

Closes #43292

This PR adds SHA-256-backed session-file fence snapshots, accepts append-only runner-owned transcript tails during the released prompt window, exports `isAgentMessage`, and expands embedded session-lock regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main throws on any session-file stat mismatch after `releaseForPrompt`, and the PR supplies real gateway logs reproducing the false takeover on long tool-using channel turns.
commands.review_followup: A narrow automated repair can add the missing persisted-entry-base validation and focused regression without changing the broader session-lock design.

target.title: fix(agents): count user turns instead of stat for embedded session takeover fence
target.body:
## Summary

The embedded attempt session-lock fence uses a `stat()`-based fingerprint (`dev`, `ino`, `size`, `mtimeNs`, `ctimeNs`) to detect "another lane queued a new user turn against this session" while the runner releases its coarse lock for the LLM prompt window. The fingerprint is a proxy that overfires: every transcript-append the runner itself performs during the released window — `appendSessionTranscriptMessageLocked` writes tool calls, tool results, and assistant replies via `acquireSessionWriteLock(..., allowReentrant: true)` — changes the file's `size`/`mtimeNs`/`ctimeNs` without going through `refreshSessionFileFence`. Long DM turns with tool calls then trip `assertSessionFileFence` and throw `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` after the reply has already been delivered, surfacing a misleading "Agent failed before reply" message on top of a successful turn.

Keep the stat fingerprint as the fast path. On mismatch, only tolerate the runner's append-only assistant/tool transcript shape; treat everything else as a real takeover. The verification requires ALL of:

- **dev/ino match** — no atomic replacement onto a new inode.
- **birthtimeNs matches** — catches the unlink+recreate-same-inode case (common on tmpfs/ext4 with rapid recreation, where dev and ino are reused but the inode itself is fresh).
- **size grew** — file is append-only between fence-set and assert.
- **bytes `[0, fenceSize)` hash identical to the fenced prefix hash** — no in-place rewrite of earlier transcript content (catches compaction-style rewrites that happen to land on a same-byte boundary).
- **bytes `[fenceSize, currentSize)` are message entries with role `assistant` or `tool`** — no new user-role turn, no malformed tail.

Anything else — replacement, shrink, in-place rewrite, new user turn, or compaction — remains a real takeover. The fence snapshot captures the prefix SHA-256 alongside the fingerprint at `releaseForPrompt` and `refreshSessionFileFence` so the slow path can verify rigorously without keeping a file handle open.

Closes #83436.
Refs #83615 (this PR addresses the EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError surface; the issue also tracks unrelated kimi-k2.6 schema and DNS-fetch failures).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Embedded runner false-positive `EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError` fires after long Telegram DM turns whose reply was already delivered, surfacing a misleading "Agent failed before reply" message on top of a successful run.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw 2026.5.19-beta.1, Linux x86_64, Node 24.13.0. Long-running Telegram DM session against `claude-opus-4-7` (~3.5 min turn with multiple `exec` and `message` tool calls), reproduced reliably before this patch. Production behavior probed via runtime-patched `dist/selection-BpjGe-Y0.js` carrying the same fix semantics, then long DM runs (41s–122s) exercised through the Telegram channel post-restart.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```
CI=true pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts
CI=true pnpm check:changed --staged=false
openclaw gateway restart
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Pre-patch failure mode, redacted runtime log from gateway (`/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-19.log`):
```
03:30:06.971  embedded run agent end: runId=bbd7d487 isError=false
03:30:07.008  embedded run prompt end: runId=bbd7d487 sessionId=<session-id> durationMs=217236
03:30:07.054  lane task error: lane=main durationMs=219867 error="EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /home/.../agents/main/sessions/<session-id>.jsonl"
03:30:07.071  Embedded agent failed before reply: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released: /home/.../<session-id>.jsonl
```
Note `isError=false` at `03:30:06.971` — the assistant turn and `message` tool delivery both succeeded — followed 47ms later by the takeover throw, surfacing as a misleading Telegram error on top of a delivered reply.

Post-patch behavior on the same gateway, four long DM runs after gateway restart at 03:46 EDT, every one delivering cleanly with zero takeover errors (redacted runtime log):
```
03:51:18  embedded run prompt end: runId=62395ffd  durationMs=122344  (no takeover)
03:52:39  embedded run prompt end: runId=67e21652  durationMs=41345   (no takeover)
04:05:05  embedded run prompt end: runId=a5994980  durationMs=101810  (no takeover)
04:07:34  embedded run prompt end: runId=a837440b  durationMs=94395   (no takeover)
```
The post-patch window contains only two "Embedded agent failed before reply" entries (at 04:05:06 and 04:07:35), both with cause `LLM request timed out` — unrelated to the takeover path. Zero `SessionTakeoverError` occurrences post-patch.

**Observed result after fix**: The renamed existing test (`rejects post-prompt writes when another owner queues a new user turn`) still asserts the takeover when a `role: "user"` entry is appended during the released window. Three new tests cover the runner's own assistant/tool appends (pass cleanly), in-place rewrite (rejected via prefix hash mismatch), and unlink+recreate-same-inode replacement (rejected via `birthtimeNs` mismatch even when the prefix bytes coincidentally match and the tail looks runner-owned). Targeted Vitest output:
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  16 passed (16)
   Start at  04:24:31
   Duration  884ms
```
`check:changed --staged=false` is clean: 0 lint warnings/errors across 8647 files, 0 import cycles, all typecheck/build/security/quality lanes pass.

**What was not tested**: Multi-process write contention on the same `sessionFile` from a different gateway process (no test infrastructure for cross-process locks in this surface). The prefix-hash check is `O(fenceSize)` I/O per fence-set; in production sessions seen so far (≤ ~150 KB) this is sub-millisecond.

## Notes

- Adds three helpers near `readSessionFileFingerprint`: `FenceSnapshot` type, `snapshotSessionFileFence` (used at `releaseForPrompt` and `refreshSessionFileFence`), and `verifyAppendOnlyRunnerOwnedExtension` (used in the slow path of `assertSessionFileFence`). No new module-level dependencies beyond `node:crypto`'s `createHash`.
- Replaces one piece of controller state (`fenceFingerprint: SessionFileFingerprint | undefined`) with `fenceSnapshot: FenceSnapshot | undefined`. No new long-lived resources (no held file handles).
- This is the second revision of #84046; the first revision (count-only check) was flagged P1 by ClawSweeper for accepting same-user-count rewrites/replacements. This revision addresses that finding directly.

target.title: fix(message): deny same-provider cross-context sends by default [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: bound `message` tool sends could still hop to a different target on the same provider by default if the model supplied an explicit `target`
- Why it matters: this allows cross-channel/context leaks on Slack, WhatsApp, iMessage, and other providers that share the outbound policy path
- What changed: same-provider cross-context sends are now denied by default; operators must opt in with `tools.message.crossContext.allowWithinProvider=true`
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): cross-provider behavior, explicit legacy override `tools.message.allowCrossContextSend`, and existing cross-context marker behavior

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts

target.title: agents: cap broad home find exec scans
target.body:
## Summary

When a request triggers a broad shell `find` over `$HOME` or `/` without an explicit tool timeout, the command can consume the entire embedded run budget and fall through to the generic 600s run timeout.

This change adds a narrow guard for that failure mode:

- cap broad home/root `find` scans at 20s when no explicit exec timeout is provided
- keep explicit operator intent intact when the command already sets `timeout`, `-prune`, `-xdev`, or `-mount`
- add system prompt guidance to prefer scoped workspace searches over broad shell scans

## Why

A recent timeout investigation showed an agent trying to locate a project directory by running a command equivalent to:

```bash
find $HOME -maxdepth 5 -type d -iname '*target*'
```

That search walked into noisy, permission-heavy trees and lasted until the embedded run hit its global 600s timeout. The user request itself was reasonable; the pathological part was letting an unbounded home-directory discovery command inherit the full run budget.

## Testing

- `pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.exec.broad-find-guard.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts -t "guides subagent workflows to avoid polling loops"`

target.title: fix(agents): classify compound read-only gateway actions correctly
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Compound gateway action names like `config.schema.lookup` are classified as mutating because `isMutatingToolCall` only checks the full action string against `READ_ONLY_ACTIONS`, and compound names are never in that set.
- Why it matters: Read-only gateway operations produce false "mutating action" classifications, which trigger unnecessary user-facing error notifications on tool failures.
- What changed: Added `lookup` to `READ_ONLY_ACTIONS` and updated the gateway/cron/canvas branch to extract the leaf verb from compound action names before checking the set.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The `_actions`-suffixed and `nodes` branches are left as-is since they do not use compound naming.

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

## Security Impact (required)

- New/changed network calls? No

target.title: fix(slack): respect userTokenReadOnly flag for read operations
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `getTokenForOperation("read")` unconditionally returns `userToken ?? botToken`, ignoring the `userTokenReadOnly` configuration flag.
- Why it matters: When `userTokenReadOnly: false` is set (to enable user-token writes), read operations still prefer the user token even when it lacks broad read scopes like `channels:history`, causing API failures.
- What changed: Read operations now check `userTokenReadOnly` — when `false`, reads prefer `botToken` (which typically has full read scopes) and fall back to `userToken` only when bot token is unavailable. When `true` (default), behavior is unchanged (user token preferred for reads).
- What did NOT change: Write-path token selection logic, token resolution in `resolveSlackAccount`, Zod schema defaults, or any other Slack action behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42752

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When `userTokenReadOnly: false`, Slack read operations (readMessages, listPins, reactions, memberInfo, emojiList) now prefer the bot token instead of the user token. This prevents failures when the user token has limited read scopes (e.g., only `identify` and `search:read`).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No — same tokens, different selection priority
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps
1. Configure Slack with both `botToken` (full scopes) and `userToken` (limited scopes: `identify`, `search:read`)
2. Set `userTokenReadOnly: false`
3. Try `readMessages` action

### Expected
- Reads use bot token (which has `channels:history`)

### Actual (before fix)
- Reads always use user token first → fails with scope error because user token lacks `channels:history`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- New tests added:
  - `uses bot token for reads when userTokenReadOnly is false`
  - `falls back to user token for reads when userTokenReadOnly is false and no bot token`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: userTokenReadOnly=true (default, unchanged), userTokenReadOnly=false (new behavior), no userToken (unchanged), no botToken with userTokenReadOnly=false (fallback)
- Edge cases checked: both tokens missing, only userToken present, only botToken present
- What you did **not** verify: live Slack API calls with real tokens

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — default behavior (`userTokenReadOnly: true`) is unchanged
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert single commit, or set `userTokenReadOnly: true` (default)
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/tools/slack-actions.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Read operations failing when `userTokenReadOnly: false` with both tokens configured

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Users who set `userTokenReadOnly: false` and relied on user token for reads may see different token used
  - Mitigation: Bot token has strictly broader scopes than user token in standard Slack setups; fallback to user token preserved when bot token is missing

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `userTokenReadOnly: true` + both tokens | userToken for reads | userToken for reads (unchanged) |
| `userTokenReadOnly: false` + both tokens | userToken for reads | **botToken for reads** |
| `userTokenReadOnly: false` + userToken only | userToken for reads | userToken for reads (fallback) |
| `userTokenReadOnly: true` + botToken only | botToken for reads | botToken for reads (unchanged) |

target.title: Docker: Move Playwright browsers to `/opt/playwright`
target.body:
Fix #42819

### Summary
Changed Playwright browser installation path from `~/.cache/ms-playwright` to `/opt/playwright` to align with FHS standards and improve persistence handling.

**Changes:**
- **Dockerfile**:
  - Set `ENV PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH=/opt/playwright`.
  - Pre-create directory with `node:node` ownership (fixes permission errors for runtime installs).
  - Added `/usr/local/bin/chromium` symlink for easier debugging.
- **Docs**: Updated Docker persistence guides to reference `/opt/playwright`.

**Benefits:**
- **FHS Compliance**: Browsers are application dependencies, not temporary cache.
- **Reliability**: Ensures the install directory is always writable by the `node` user, even if the browser isn't pre-installed at build time.
- **Simplicity**: Easier to mount a persistent volume to a fixed system path than a hidden dotfile path.

### Change Type
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Chore/infra

### Verification
- [x] Built image with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER=1`: Verified chromium installs to `/opt/playwright`.
- [x] Built image without browser: Verified `/opt/playwright` exists and is writable by `node` user at runtime.


```
$docker build  -t openclaw/openclaw:20260311 --build-arg OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER=1 -f Dockerfile  .
```

```
$ docker run --rm -it openclaw/openclaw:20260311 bash
node@3dc553ab2990:/app$ ls -lt /opt/playwright
total 12
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Mar 11 04:05 chromium_headless_shell-1208
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Mar 11 04:05 chromium-1208
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mar 11 04:05 ffmpeg-1011
```

target.title: fix(cron): prevent duplicate job execution after gateway restart (#42640)
target.body:
Fixes #42640

## Summary

After gateway restart, the cron scheduler re-executed jobs that already ran before the restart, causing duplicate deliveries. The root cause: `isRunnableJob` treated stale `nextRunAtMs <= now` slots as due without checking whether they had already been executed.

## Changes

- Add `hasAlreadyExecutedScheduledSlot` guard in `timer.ts`: if `lastRunAtMs >= nextRunAtMs`, the expired slot was already executed — skip it
- Preserves missed-slot fallthrough so genuinely missed jobs still get caught up

## Testing

- Added regression test: `does not re-run an already-executed stale due slot on restart`
- 501 tests pass across cron module

target.title: feat(pairing): add configurable pairingMessage text per channel (#41058)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds optional `pairingMessage` config to iMessage and Telegram channel configs, allowing operators to customize or suppress OpenClaw branding in pairing challenge messages sent to unknown senders.

Fixes #41058

## Problem

When `dmPolicy: pairing` is set, every unknown sender receives a hardcoded message exposing:
1. The OpenClaw brand name
2. The channel platform ("Your iMessage sender id:" / "Your Telegram user id:")
3. The full CLI invocation syntax (`openclaw pairing approve imessage CODE`)

No config option exists to override any part of this. The only workaround is patching compiled dist bundles after every update — not sustainable for white-label or privacy-conscious deployments.

## What changed

- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfig` type with optional fields (`header`, `senderIdLabel`, `codeLabel`, `footer`, `showCliHint`). Update `buildPairingReply` to use config when provided.
- `src/config/types.imessage.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts`: Add `pairingMessage?: PairingMessageConfig`
- `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`: Add `PairingMessageConfigSchema`, wire into `IMessageAccountSchemaBase` and `TelegramAccountSchemaBase`
- `src/imessage/monitor/monitor-provider.ts`: Wire `pairingMessage` config into `issuePairingChallenge` via existing `buildReplyText` hook
- `src/telegram/dm-access.ts`: Pass `pairingMessage` through `enforceTelegramDmAccess`, wire into `issuePairingChallenge`
- `src/pairing/pairing-messages.test.ts`: Add tests for all config overrides and backward compat

## Fully backward compatible

All `PairingMessageConfig` fields are optional. Existing deployments with no `pairingMessage` config see zero behavior change.

## Example config

```yaml
channels:
  imessage:
    pairingMessage:
      header: 'Sender verification required.'
      senderIdLabel: 'Your sender ID:'
      footer: 'Share this code with your contact to complete verification.'
      showCliHint: false
```

## Notes

Discord, Signal, Slack, and LINE call sites follow the same pattern and can be extended in a follow-up if the team prefers to land iMessage/Telegram first.

## AI Disclosure
This PR was authored with AI assistance (OpenClaw/Jordan + Neo agents).
Testing: Fully tested — unit tests in `pairing-messages.test.ts` cover all config branches including edge cases; CI passes (types, lint, full test suite on Linux/Windows/macOS).
The authors have reviewed and understand all code changes in this PR.

target.title: fix: route embedded tool calls through in-process dispatch (#40237)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #40237 (also resolves #5703 and #6508 which were circular-duped shut).

When agent tools like `cron.run`, `cron.list`, `cron.add` are called from within an active LLM session, `callGatewayTool` opens a **new WebSocket connection** to the same gateway. Because the gateway's Node.js event loop is busy processing the current LLM turn, the second WS connection sits in queue and times out after 10s.

This PR routes embedded tool calls through the existing in-process dispatch path (`dispatchGatewayMethod`) when running inside the gateway process, bypassing WS entirely. External CLI tool calls continue to use WS unchanged.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/local-dispatch.ts`** (new): lightweight registry that the gateway server populates at startup. Exposes `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` which returns a promise when in-process dispatch is available, or `undefined` when running outside the gateway (e.g. CLI).

- **`src/gateway/server-plugins.ts`**: registers `dispatchGatewayMethod` as the local dispatcher when `setFallbackGatewayContext` is called during gateway startup.

- **`src/agents/tools/gateway.ts`**: `callGatewayTool` now checks `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` first. If available (inside gateway), dispatches in-process with zero WS overhead. Otherwise falls through to the existing WS path.

## How it works

```
CLI tool call (external):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns undefined → WS path (unchanged)

Embedded tool call (inside gateway LLM session):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns Promise → dispatchGatewayMethod
    → handleGatewayRequest (in-process, no WS) → immediate response
```

The `dispatchGatewayMethod` function already exists and is battle-tested — it's used by plugin subagent runtime for sessions, agent dispatch, etc. This PR simply makes it available to agent tools via the registry pattern.

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests for `local-dispatch.ts` (registry behavior, reset, error propagation)
- [x] Integration tests in `gateway.test.ts` verifying:
  - Embedded tool calls use in-process dispatch (no WS `callGateway` mock called)
  - External tool calls fall back to WS when no dispatcher registered
  - `undefined` params correctly normalized to `{}`
- [x] Existing `server-plugins.test.ts` tests pass (5/5)
- [x] Type check clean (`pnpm tsgo`)
- [x] Lint/format clean (`pnpm check`)

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: feat(memory): add excludeDirs to filter directories from memory indexing [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an `excludeDirs` config option to the memory search indexer that prevents junk `.md` files from directories like `node_modules`, `.git`, `__pycache__`, `venv`, etc. from polluting the memory index when using `extraPaths`.

Refs: #6109

## Problem

When using `extraPaths` to include project directories in memory search, the indexer recursively walks all subdirectories and picks up `.md` files from `node_modules`, `.git`, `__pycache__`, `venv`, and other junk directories. This pollutes the memory index with irrelevant content and degrades search quality.

## Solution

Added `excludeDirs` — a list of directory basenames to skip during recursive directory walks. Sensible defaults are applied out of the box:

```
node_modules, .git, __pycache__, .venv, venv, dist, build, .next, .nuxt, vendor, .tox, egg-info
```

User-provided entries **extend** (not replace) the default list.

### Config example

```yaml
agents:
  defaults:
    memorySearch:
      extraPaths:
        - ~/projects/my-app
      excludeDirs:
        - .cache
        - tmp
```

The above would exclude `.cache` and `tmp` **in addition to** all the built-in defaults.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Added `excludeDirs` to schema |
| `src/config/types.tools.ts` | Added `excludeDirs` to `MemorySearchConfig` type |
| `src/config/schema.labels.ts` | Added UI label |
| `src/config/schema.help.ts` | Added help text |
| `src/agents/memory-search.ts` | Added to `ResolvedMemorySearchConfig`, default list, merge logic |
| `src/memory/internal.ts` | `walkDir` + `listMemoryFiles` accept and apply `excludeDirs` |
| `src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` | Pass `excludeDirs` from settings |
| `src/memory/manager.ts` | Include `excludeDirs` in status output |
| `src/memory/types.ts` | Added to `MemoryProviderStatus` |
| `src/memory/internal.test.ts` | 3 new tests for excludeDirs filtering |

## Testing

- 3 new tests added covering: excludeDirs in extraPaths, excludeDirs in default memory dir, and empty excludeDirs set
- All existing tests pass (`pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 16/16 internal tests, 764/764 config tests, 14/14 memory-search tests)

## Notes

- 🤖 AI-assisted (Claude) — fully tested
- The `excludeDirs` filter applies to **both** the default `memory/` directory and any `extraPaths` directories
- Default excludeDirs are sensible out of the box — no configuration needed for the common case

target.title: feat(cli-output): tool-use injection + rolling-timer indicator for claude-stream-json backends
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** When Claude calls a tool mid-turn on the `claude-cli` backend, the assistant text stream just pauses — often for tens of seconds on `Read` / `Bash` / `Grep` / `WebFetch`. The end user sees a frozen typing indicator and no signal about which tool is running.
- **Why it matters.** Looks like the gateway hung; users abandon turns that are still in flight. Tool calls live in `stream_event` records, never in `assistant_text` deltas, so the existing assistant-stream consumer never surfaces them.
- **What changed.** Two complementary surfaces on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` (gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`):
  1. **In-stream textual marker** via `onAssistantDelta` — `\n\n[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail\n` followed by a rolling `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator that refreshes every 8s, stripped on the next `text_delta` or on turn end.
  2. **Structured `stream: "tool"` agent event** (`{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}`) via a new optional `onToolEvent` callback — consumed by `cli-runner/execute.ts` and forwarded to the agent event bus for downstream tool-tile UIs.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect({backend, providerId})` — no impact on non-Claude CLI backends. Existing `onAssistantDelta` callers are unaffected. `onToolEvent` is opt-in. No tool-output / tool-result event in this PR (the result is reflected in claude's next `text_delta` and the timer-strip handles UX). Tool args sanitized via `sanitizeToolArgs` before either path.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** tool calls during a Claude turn now produce both an in-stream textual marker (with rolling 8s timer indicator) and a structured `stream: "tool"` agent event, when `backend.jsonlDialect === "claude-stream-json"` or `providerId === "claude-cli"`.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142, Opus 4.7, live OpenClaw gateway driving the `claude-cli` backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`; live Telegram turn that invokes one or more tools (Read / Bash / Grep / WebFetch); observe the textual marker, the 8s tick refresh, and the marker stripping plus `\n\n` separator when assistant text resumes; verify the `stream: "tool"` agent event in `emitAgentEvent` logs.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Telegram screenshot of a live turn (rolling-timer rendered to the end-user surface): ![telegram rolling-timer](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-82285/.proof/telegram-rolling-timer.jpg). Every `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Bash: …` line in the screenshot is one `emitTool` firing in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` against `claude-cli`.
  - Typecheck clean: `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`.
  - The markers are an in-flight overlay delivered via `onAssistantDelta`, not persisted to `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl` (which stores claude's structured `result`) or `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` at default verbosity — Telegram is the canonical capture surface.
- **Observed result after fix:** tool calls render as `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail` blocks in the assistant text, with the live timer ticking every 8s. When claude resumes streaming text, the timer is stripped and the text continues from a clean `\n\n` boundary. Agent-event consumers receive a structured `{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}` for each tool call.
- **What was not tested:** end-to-end against a dashboard / external listener for the `stream: "tool"` event surface (event is emitted, visible in `emitAgentEvent` logs, but no downstream UI verified beyond that); Linux/macOS execution (developed on Windows only); bundled MCP tool paths (`server_tool_use` / `mcp_tool_use` content-block types are matched in the tracker but I have not driven a live MCP-tool turn through it); focused unit test for `createClaudeToolUseTracker` is not added yet (see Regression Test Plan).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Adds opt-in `onToolEvent` callback on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` + in-stream textual marker over the existing `onAssistantDelta`.
- Overlap to coordinate: #80046 (CLI tool-event bridge). Maintainers' judgment on whether inline rolling markers ride alongside or after the structured bridge.

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The condition this addresses (no tool-call UX during turns) is a property of the Claude CLI JSONL surface (tool calls live in `stream_event`, never in `assistant_text` deltas), not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` — add a `describe("tool-use tracker", …)` block.
- **Scenario locked in:** simulated `stream_event` sequence — `message_start` → `content_block_start (tool_use, name: "Read")` → 3× `content_block_delta (input_json_delta partial)` → `content_block_stop` — asserts the resulting `ClaudeToolEvent` carries the reassembled `args.file_path`, the right `itemId`, and that the parser's `assistantText` now includes the `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Read: <path>` line.
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode (regression of partial-JSON accumulation, wrong `content_block_stop` dispatch, verbose-gating drift) is fully exercisable by calling the parser's `push()` with a canned JSONL transcript — no live claude needed. Runs in ms.
- **Honest gap:** I have **not** added this test yet. Behaviour is exercised live in my local environment but a frozen-transcript unit test belongs with this PR before flipping out of draft. Flagging as a must-add before un-drafting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** partial-JSON accumulation in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` could drop or corrupt tool args if `content_block_stop` fires before all `input_json_delta` chunks arrive.
  - **Mitigation:** tracker holds the entry until `content_block_stop`, then parses `entry.input ?? entry.partialJson` via `parseClaudeToolArgs`. The pending unit test (`cli-output.test.ts`) locks in the contract for partial-JSON reassembly.
- **Risk:** rolling-timer `setInterval` leaks if a `content_block_stop` arrives but no subsequent `text_delta` fires (e.g. turn aborts mid-tool).
  - **Mitigation:** `clearToolKeepalive()` is also called on `result`, on `finish()`, and on the next `text_delta`. The parser closure owns the interval handle and explicitly clears it on every terminal path.
- **Risk:** tool args contain sensitive content (file paths, credentials in `WebFetch` URLs, etc.) and surface in both Telegram and agent events.
  - **Mitigation:** `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates and redacts known-sensitive keys before either emission path. Verbose gating (`shouldInjectToolInlineMarkers`) lets sessions opt out of inline markers without losing the structured event.
- **Risk:** the inline marker rides on `onAssistantDelta` — any consumer that strict-parses assistant text as Markdown could choke on the `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator.
  - **Mitigation:** marker uses a deliberately conservative `\n\n[…] 🛠️ …\n` shape that's Markdown-safe (Telegram renders it verbatim). Consumers wanting strict text can ignore by subscribing only to the structured `stream: "tool"` event.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — same allowed-tools set; `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates args before emission.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same tools claude already executes; this PR only renders that they're in flight.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — tracker reads `stream_event` records the parser already processes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`; non-Claude backends unaffected.
- `onToolEvent` is an additive optional callback — existing parser callers don't need to wire it up.
- No new config, no new env vars. Disable by not subscribing to the new event surface.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: fix(cron): capture originating session/agent on the cron wake tool call
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** The cron `wake` MCP tool forwards only `{mode, text}` to the gateway, so every wake enqueues against the heartbeat/main default. Wakes scheduled from a non-main session (Telegram thread, Discord channel, multi-agent setup) silently route to the wrong conversation lane — on CLI runtimes the fired wake spawns a fresh session and burns tokens generating output nothing can route back; on embedded the wake fires the agent's default session, not the originating one.
- **What changed.** Wake tool resolves `opts.agentSessionKey` via the existing `resolveInternalSessionKey` / `resolveSessionAgentId` pattern (mirrors the `add` action at L569-583) and threads `sessionKey` + `agentId` through `WakeParamsSchema` → gateway handler → `context.cron.wake` → `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeat`. Explicit params on the tool call take precedence over inferred values.
- **CLI runtime coverage (claude-cli + claude-cli-interactive).** No CLI-specific changes — the runner's `useResume` is already auto-derived from `cliSessionId` + `backend.resumeArgs` presence (`src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts:276-277`). Once the wake's `sessionKey` reaches the cron service, the heartbeat-runner targets the right session lane → the cron isolated agent resolves the right `sessionEntry` → `getCliSessionId` returns that session's claude-side id → `--resume <sessionId>` flips on automatically for any backend that declares `resumeArgs` (both Claude backends do). Same path works for Codex, Gemini-CLI, etc.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** `WakeParamsSchema` still permits `additionalProperties: true`. Pre-existing call sites that omit origin still route to the dep's default (backwards compatible). Subagent session keys reject early with `unwakeable-session-key` (upstream guard, preserved). Channel/thread/topic capture on the job's `delivery` block stays a follow-up — this PR's session-key bridge unblocks the wake-side routing without touching the delivery contract.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** cron `wake` invoked by an agent inside a non-main session threads the originating session/agent through the wake gateway call so the system event lands on that session's queue and the targeted heartbeat picks it up. CLI runtimes (both `claude-cli` headless and `claude-cli-interactive`) consume the resolved session through the existing `useResume` auto-derivation, so the woken claude process resumes the originating conversation rather than spawning fresh.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, AJV-compiled TypeBox validators, vitest 4.1.6. Live harness in `scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts` drives the patched gateway wake handler (`validateWakeParams` + `isSubagentSessionKey` guard) through the patched `cron.wake()` / `timer.ts` with logging deps that print every `enqueueSystemEvent` / `requestHeartbeat` call.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run src/cron/service/wake-origin.test.ts src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts && pnpm exec tsx scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts`.
- **Evidence after fix:** unit-test suite — `Test Files 3 passed (3) / Tests 82 passed (82) / Duration 8.05s`. Live harness stdout (synthetic ids; real chat identifiers redacted at source — the script never references real ones):

  ```
  === non-main session + non-default agent (the bug-fix case) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"follow up on report","sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="follow up on report"  opts={"sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake","sessionKey":"agent:coding:telegram:<chat-id-redacted>:topic:<topic-id-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === no origin (backwards-compatible default routing) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"ping"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="ping"  opts=undefined
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === next-heartbeat + sessionKey collapses to targeted-immediate ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"next-heartbeat","text":"check the queue","sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="check the queue"  opts={"sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake","sessionKey":"agent:coding:discord:<thread-redacted>","agentId":"coding"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}

  === subagent sessionKey rejected by gateway handler guard ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"wake my subagent","sessionKey":"subagent:scratch:<id-redacted>"}
  < wake result:  ok=false  payload=undefined
  < wake error:   {"code":"INVALID_REQUEST","message":"wake sessionKey cannot target a subagent session"}

  === whitespace-only origin falls through (defence-in-depth) ===
  > wake params: {"mode":"now","text":"x","sessionKey":"   ","agentId":"\t"}
  [gateway/cron] enqueueSystemEvent  text="x"  opts=undefined
  [gateway/heartbeat] requestHeartbeat  {"source":"manual","intent":"immediate","reason":"wake"}
  < wake result:  ok=true  payload={"ok":true}
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** non-main wake calls hit `enqueueSystemEvent(text, {sessionKey, agentId})` and `requestHeartbeat({source: "manual", intent: "immediate", reason: "wake", sessionKey, agentId})` on the originating lane, not the heartbeat/main default. No-origin calls preserve the pre-fix `enqueueSystemEvent(text, undefined)` + `requestHeartbeat({source, intent, reason})` shape. `mode: "next-heartbeat" + sessionKey` collapses to targeted-immediate because the regularly-scheduled heartbeat fires for the agent's main session, never peeking the targeted lane's queue. Subagent session keys reject at the gateway handler with `INVALID_REQUEST`. Whitespace-only origin trims to undefined and falls through to default routing (defence-in-depth at both the handler and `wake()`).
- **What was not tested:** live end-to-end Telegram thread → cron wake → CLI claude run → reply landing back in the thread. The harness pins the gateway-side routing contract; the CLI `useResume` auto-derivation is exercised by existing CLI runner tests on `--resume` argv assembly. A full live-Telegram capture is a Mantis follow-up (it needs `delivery.{channel,threadId,topicId}` capture on the cron job, which is deliberately split into a separate PR per the scope boundary above).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Closes the upstream half of #46886 and #64556.
- Related (downstream slices): #70268, #57199, #82767, #79869, #63096. This PR fixes the upstream origin-capture they all silently assume.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** existing in-process callers of `wake({mode, text})` keep working only if origin remains optional.
  - **Mitigation:** `sessionKey` / `agentId` optional; omitted (or whitespace-only) falls through to the dep's default. Regression test pins both branches.
- **Risk:** new `NonEmptyString` slots on the schema reject empty-string origin from existing callers.
  - **Mitigation:** existing callers don't send these fields. `additionalProperties: true` unchanged so forward-compat metadata still passes.
- **Risk:** inferred `agentId` could differ from a wake intended for a different agent (e.g. operator scheduling a wake for `coding` from `main`).
  - **Mitigation:** explicit `sessionKey` / `agentId` params on the tool call override the inferred values. The implicit capture is the default for the "wake my own session" case.
- **Risk:** a CLI-backend wake that doesn't have a prior claude session on disk (`cliSessionId === undefined`) would skip the `--resume` plumbing and start fresh.
  - **Mitigation:** that's the correct behaviour — fresh-session wakes have no history to resume. The auto-derivation gate (`useResume = Boolean(cliSessionId && resolvedSessionId && backend.resumeArgs)`) means existing sessions resume and brand-new sessions don't try to resume a session that doesn't exist.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — session keys and agent ids are not credentials.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `wake` action; only the routing target changes.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — wake events still confined to in-process session queues; routing now goes to the correct queue.
