target.title: Telegram: add reaction notification wake policy
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram reaction events were queued as passive context, so reaction-only inputs did not wake the routed agent session.
- Why it matters: operators may want selected notification events to wake a routed session, but making every accepted reaction wake by default is a compatibility change.
- What changed: accepted Telegram reaction events now include stable `reaction_key` values for standard and custom emoji and route through shared `notificationWake.reactions` policy.
- Compatibility boundary: reaction events stay queue-only by default; set `channels.telegram.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` to request immediate routed heartbeat wakes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #47677
- Related: #83611
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: accepted Telegram reaction-only events now enqueue stable reaction-key system events and wake the routed session only when the resolved shared notification wake policy is `wake`. Default policy remains `queue`, preserving existing reaction notification compatibility.
- Real environment tested: Redacted OpenClaw development checkout on PR head `1bde45dcc7`, plus the earlier live Telegram Bot API canary for bot identity/message reachability. No secrets, usernames, chat titles, local paths, or raw IDs are included here.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm config:channels:check`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm tsgo:prod`
  - `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`
  - `git diff --check`
- Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal verification from PR head:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  159 passed (159)

pnpm config:channels:check
passed

pnpm config:docs:check
OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256

pnpm tsgo:prod
passed

pnpm test:contracts:plugins
Test Files  62 passed (62)
Tests  857 passed (857)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check
passed
```

Supplemental earlier live canary proof, redacted: Telegram `getMe` succeeded and a canary message posted to the configured target. This proves Bot API reachability, but not a production reaction update wake.
- Observed result after fix: The shared helper queues notification system events by default, skips enqueue for `off`, wakes only for resolved `wake`, preserves dedupe no-wake behavior, and resolves account policy before channel, agent, channel defaults, and global defaults. Telegram handler tests prove queue-by-default, opt-in wake, custom emoji stable keys, trust downgrade, authorization/routing preservation, and no wake when enqueue deduplicates.
- What was not tested: I did not capture a live Telegram Desktop reaction update waking a production routed session after this policy patch. The PR still has the `mantis: telegram-visible-proof` label so maintainer/Mantis proof can cover that external surface.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current main enqueues accepted Telegram reaction system events and returns without requesting a heartbeat wake.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Telegram `message_reaction` handling enqueued system events and then returned without a policy-controlled wake path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests asserted enqueue behavior but did not assert notification wake policy, default queue compatibility, or deduped no-wake behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram custom emoji reactions were not represented in the event text because only standard emoji reactions were collected.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Contract test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files: `src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`, `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
- Scenario the test should lock in: accepted standard/custom emoji reactions enqueue stable reaction-key events, stay queue-only by default, wake only under `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`, and do not wake when enqueue deduplicates.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the shared helper owns policy precedence and heartbeat behavior; the Telegram handler harness owns reaction authorization/routing/enqueue behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram reaction notifications that pass existing access controls enqueue system events by default. Operators can opt into immediate routed wakes with `channels.telegram.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`. System event text includes `reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>` or `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>` so preference/prompt text can assign meaning to specific reactions without adding per-emoji channel schema.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
[Telegram reaction] -> [system event queued] -> [processed on next scheduled/explicit wake]

Opt-in wake:
[Telegram reaction] -> [notificationWake.reactions = wake] -> [system event queued] -> [heartbeat wake] -> [agent sees reaction event]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: redacted local development environment
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram handler unit harness and shared notification system-event helper
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.telegram.reactionNotifications: "all"`, `dmPolicy: "open"`, `allowFrom: ["*"]`, and opt-in wake case with `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`

### Steps

1. Run shared notification wake helper tests plus focused Telegram reaction and dispatch tests.
2. Run generated config metadata and docs checks.
3. Run production typecheck and plugin contract tests.
4. Run format and whitespace checks.

### Expected

- Default reaction notifications enqueue without wake.
- `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` wakes the routed session after enqueue.
- `notificationWake.reactions: "off"` skips notification enqueue.
- Deduped notification events do not wake.
- Typecheck, generated config checks, plugin contracts, format, and whitespace checks pass.

### Actual

- Passed locally on PR head `1bde45dcc7`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: queue-by-default reaction notification, explicit wake policy, off policy, account policy precedence, dedupe no-wake, standard emoji stable key, custom emoji stable key, existing authorization/routing cases.
- Edge cases checked: reaction removals ignored, `own`/`all`/`off` reaction modes, bot-user reactions ignored, forum/general topic routing remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live Telegram Desktop user reaction update waking a production routed session.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Yes, optional `notificationWake.reactions` policy surface
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: reaction notifications could start an agent turn unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: default policy is `queue`; immediate wake requires explicit `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` and remains gated by existing Telegram sender/access controls.
- Risk: shared policy precedence could surprise users with layered defaults.
  - Mitigation: tests cover account over channel over agent/global defaults, and docs state the channel-level setting.

target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: fix: preserve code block indentation in duplicate block collapsing
target.body:
Closes #41699

## Problem

`collapseConsecutiveDuplicateBlocks()` in `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` uses `block.trim()` when rebuilding text after removing consecutive duplicate blocks. This strips leading whitespace (indentation) from code blocks, making JSON/Python/YAML output harder to read.

## Fix

Replace `result.push(block.trim())` with `result.push(block)`.

The comparison logic already uses a separate `normalizeBlock()` that trims and collapses whitespace for dedup purposes — the output block itself does not need to be trimmed.

## Impact

Only affects messages where consecutive duplicate text blocks exist AND the text contains indented code blocks. The early-return path (`result.length === blocks.length → return text`) is unchanged, so messages without duplicates are not affected at all.

target.title: fix(session): defer systemSent persistence until LLM response succeeds
target.body:
## Problem

When the first LLM call in a session fails (e.g. insufficient API credits, provider timeout), `systemSent: true` is already persisted to disk by `ensureSkillSnapshot()` in `session-updates.ts`. All subsequent sessions then skip re-sending the system prompt (including tool definitions), causing **"Tool not found" errors for every tool except `read`**.

This is root cause #2 from #41462 — the corrupted session state path. The reporter confirmed that deleting all session files and even rebooting did not help, because `systemSent: true` was written back to disk on every new session attempt before the LLM call.

## Fix

1. **`session-updates.ts`**: Remove `systemSent: true` from the object persisted to disk in `ensureSkillSnapshot()`. The in-memory flag is still returned so the caller knows to include the system prompt in the LLM request.

2. **`agent-runner.ts`**: Persist `systemSent: true` to disk only after a successful LLM response, using `updateSessionStoreEntry()`.

This ensures that a failed first call leaves `systemSent` unset on disk, so the next attempt will re-send the full system prompt with tool definitions.

## Test

Added `session-updates-systemsent.test.ts` that verifies:
- `ensureSkillSnapshot()` returns `systemSent: true` in memory (for the caller)
- The on-disk session store does **not** contain `systemSent: true` after `ensureSkillSnapshot()`

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/session-updates.ts` | Remove premature `systemSent: true` from disk write |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` | Persist `systemSent: true` after successful LLM response |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/session-updates-systemsent.test.ts` | Regression test |

Fixes #41462

target.title: fix: GGML crash on shutdown, cooldown probe bypass, startup stagger
target.body:
## Summary

- **GGML Metal crash** (`src/runtime.ts`): Gateway shutdown via `process.exit()` triggers C++ atexit handlers where `ggml_metal_rsets_free` races with a background init thread, causing SIGABRT. New `createGatewayRuntime()` uses `process._exit()` to skip atexit handlers — safe because the gateway completes all cleanup (server close, lock release, signal removal) before exit.

- **Cooldown probe bypass** (`src/agents/model-fallback.ts`): Three paths allowed API calls to rate-limited providers during active cooldown: `PROBE_MARGIN_MS` (2-min early probe), non-primary rate-limit bypass, and `allowTransientCooldownProbe` flag. All removed for active cooldowns. Safety-valve probing preserved only for expired/corrupt cooldown data (Infinity, NaN, null) with explicit `allowTransientCooldownProbe: true` so the embedded runner can use cooldowned profiles.

- **Startup stagger** (`src/cron/service/timer.ts`): Adds configurable `startupStaggerMs` delay between sequential missed-job executions on gateway restart, preventing rate-limit saturation from burst catch-up. Registered in zod schema (`src/config/zod-schema.ts`) and type definition.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/runtime.ts` | Add `createGatewayRuntime()` with `process._exit()` |
| `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts` | Use `createGatewayRuntime()` for gateway |
| `src/agents/model-fallback.ts` | Remove probe margin, non-primary bypass; add `allowTransientCooldownProbe` for safety-valve |
| `src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts` | 15 tests covering all cooldown probe scenarios |
| `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` | Update 2 tests for new skip-during-cooldown behavior |
| `src/cron/service/timer.ts` | Add `resolveStartupStaggerMs()` + integrate into catchup plan |
| `src/config/zod-schema.ts` | Register `startupStaggerMs` in strict cron schema |
| `src/config/types.cron.ts` | Add `startupStaggerMs` to `CronConfig` type |

## Test plan

- [x] All 72 model-fallback tests pass (probe + main)
- [x] `pnpm build` succeeds with no warnings
- [x] Safety-valve tests verify `allowTransientCooldownProbe: true` for expired/corrupt cooldown
- [x] Active-cooldown tests verify no probing (30min, 1min remaining)
- [x] Throttle tests verify 30s probe interval
- [ ] Manual: verify gateway shutdown no longer crashes on Metal-enabled hosts

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(discord): preserve content-type and skip optimization for animated/WebP images
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Discord media uploads were sending files without a MIME content-type, causing some clients/bots to misidentify the file format. Additionally, WebP images were being converted to JPEG (losing quality) and animated images (GIF, WebP, APNG) were being converted/optimized, destroying animation frames.
- **Why it matters:** Users sending WebP images or animated GIFs/APNGs via Discord and the web provider received degraded or broken output — silent data-loss bugs.
- **What changed:** `toDiscordFileBlob` now accepts and forwards a `contentType` to the `Blob` constructor; `loadWebMediaInternal` skips image optimization for GIF, WebP, and any animated image (detected via sharp's `pages` metadata); a new `isAnimatedImage` helper was added to `image-ops.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** Non-animated JPEG/PNG optimization paths are unchanged; no other channels are affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #41256
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- WebP images sent via Discord or the web provider are no longer silently converted to JPEG.
- Animated GIFs and animated WebP/APNG files are no longer passed through the image optimizer (which would strip frames).
- Discord uploads now include the correct MIME type in the `Blob`, so recipients see the right file format.
- Error messages for oversized GIF/WebP/animated images now include a more specific label (`GIF`, `WebP`, `Animated image`) instead of the generic `Media`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel: Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Send an animated GIF or a WebP image to a Discord channel via OpenClaw.
2. Observe the uploaded file in Discord.

### Expected

- Animated GIFs play in Discord.
- WebP images display as WebP (not JPEG).
- File MIME type is correctly reported.

### Actual (before fix)

- WebP images were converted to JPEG.
- Animated WebP images lost their animation frames.
- `Blob` had no `type`, so Discord received files with an unknown MIME type.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — new tests added in `src/discord/send.shared.test.ts`, `src/media/image-ops.helpers.test.ts`, and `src/web/media.test.ts` covering all three fix areas.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: _fill in_
- Edge cases checked: static WebP (should still skip optimization), single-frame GIF
- What you did **not** verify: live Discord upload end-to-end (depends on bot credentials)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert `src/web/media.ts` to re-enable optimization for WebP/animated; revert `src/discord/send.shared.ts` to drop content-type from `Blob`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/web/media.ts`, `src/discord/send.shared.ts`, `src/discord/send.components.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: WebP images silently downgraded to JPEG; animated images displayed as still frames; Discord upload MIME type shown as `application/octet-stream`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Large WebP or animated images that previously would have been resized/optimized will now be passed through at full size and may hit the cap limit sooner.
  - Mitigation: The existing size-cap check still applies; users get a clear error (`WebP` / `Animated image` label) rather than silent conversion.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: fix(config): warn when configured model primary is not in catalog
target.body:
## Summary

When a user sets `agents.defaults.model.primary` or `agents.list[].model` to a model name that does not exist in the runtime catalog (e.g. `openai-codex/gpt-5.4-codex` before the Codex provider ships it), the config is accepted silently. Every cron job and heartbeat then fails at runtime with `FailoverError: Unknown model`, and the user has no indication why without reading raw error logs.

This adds a lightweight validation pass that checks configured model primaries against the loaded model catalog and surfaces warnings through `openclaw doctor`. Warnings, not errors, because forward-compat model names are intentional and the model may appear later.

## What changed

- `collectModelConfigWarnings(config, catalog)` in `src/config/validation.ts`: iterates `agents.defaults.model.primary` and each `agents.list[].model`, resolves them through the existing `resolveModelRefFromString` path, and returns `ConfigValidationIssue[]` when any ref is absent from the catalog.
- `src/commands/doctor.ts`: loads the model catalog once and calls `collectModelConfigWarnings`. Reuses the same catalog instance for the existing `hooks.gmail.model` check to avoid a redundant `loadModelCatalog` call.

## What did NOT change

No runtime behavior, no config schema, no model resolution logic, no failover paths. This is purely an advisory check surfaced through `doctor`.

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39811
- Related #39818
- Related #37623

## Validation

```
pnpm vitest run src/config/validation.model-warnings.test.ts  # 6/6 pass
pnpm build                                                     # clean
pnpm check                                                     # clean
```

## Tests

6 targeted tests in `src/config/validation.model-warnings.test.ts`:
- valid model in catalog produces no warning
- unknown default model primary triggers warning
- string-form model config triggers warning
- unknown per-agent model triggers warning
- no model configured produces no warning
- empty catalog still warns (cannot validate)

target.title: fix(nvidia): update Nemotron 3 Super contextWindow to 1M per NVIDIA spec
target.body:
## Summary

Updates `nvidia/nemotron-3-super-120b-a12b` in `extensions/nvidia/openclaw.plugin.json` from a 262144 (256k) `contextWindow` to 1000000 (1M) to match NVIDIA's published spec for the model.

NVIDIA's [Nemotron 3 Super technical blog](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/introducing-nemotron-3-super-an-open-hybrid-mamba-transformer-moe-for-agentic-reasoning/) states explicitly: *"1M token context window for long-term agent coherence, cross-document reasoning, and multi-step task planning."* This is enabled by the hybrid Mamba-Transformer SSM architecture (Mamba-2 layers provide linear-time complexity over sequence length, making 1M practical rather than theoretical).

The other three models in the same catalog (`moonshotai/kimi-k2.5`, `minimaxai/minimax-m2.5`, `z-ai/glm5`) match upstream specs and are unchanged. Scoped intentionally narrow; happy to file follow-ups for catalog refresh (Kimi K2.6, GLM-5.1, MiniMax M2.7, DeepSeek V4) as separate PRs.

## Verification

The catalog `contextWindow` is the authoritative value at runtime — per-agent `models.json` overrides cannot raise it above what the bundled plugin declares. So a stale value here silently caps real workloads.

Real behavior proof:

Behavior addressed: stale `contextWindow` clamps Nemotron 3 Super at 256k regardless of per-agent overrides; long-context workflows (large codebase analysis, document corpora) that the model was explicitly designed for cannot use the full window.
Real environment tested: macOS 24.6.0, openclaw 2026.5.18 (`50a2481`), Node 25.8.0, NVIDIA NIM (`https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1`, free tier API key)
Exact steps or command run after this patch: edited `extensions/nvidia/openclaw.plugin.json` to set `contextWindow: 1000000` for `nvidia/nemotron-3-super-120b-a12b`; re-ran `openclaw models list --json` against the patched installation.
Evidence after fix: with the upstream catalog at 262144, agent-side `models.json` overrides up to `1000000` were silently capped at 200000 in `openclaw models list --json` output. After the patch the catalog value flows through.
Observed result after fix: `openclaw models list --json` reports `contextWindow: 1000000` for `nvidia/nemotron-3-super-120b-a12b`. Other three NVIDIA models unchanged.
What was not tested: did not exercise an actual >256k token completion against the NIM endpoint (would burn free-tier credits); did not run `pnpm test:extension nvidia` (data-only JSON change with no code or schema impact). Both are good follow-ups before merge if desired.

target.title: fix(gateway): bind to loopback alongside tailnet/custom IPs for internal calls
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When using `--bind tailnet` or a custom IP, the gateway only listens on that specific address. Internal operations (`sessions_spawn`, CLI probes, subagent RPC) hardcode connections to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789`, which fails because nothing is listening on loopback.
- **Why it matters:** Users running OpenClaw headless/remote with Tailscale cannot use subagents or session spawning — core orchestration features break silently. Multiple users have reported this as a regression affecting real deployments.
- **What changed:** `resolveGatewayListenHosts()` now returns both the specified IP and `127.0.0.1` when the bind host is a specific IP (not `0.0.0.0` which already binds all interfaces). The `canBind()` helper is hoisted for reuse across all branches.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No auth policy changes. No changes to how `0.0.0.0` (LAN mode) or `127.0.0.1` (loopback mode) behave. No client-side RPC target changes.

Fixes #24011
Fixes #30589
Fixes #50607

## Related

- #29871 — alternative approach (client-side forceLoopback), superseded by this fix
- #30618 — related tailnet RPC target fix
- #50751 — related CLI gateway host resolution

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Verification

Ran:

```bash
pnpm test src/gateway/net.test.ts
```

Passed: `6/6` tests covering loopback, LAN, and specific-IP modes with various bindability scenarios.

Live verification:
- Gateway listens on both Tailscale IP and localhost
- `openclaw channels status --probe` succeeds with tailnet binding
- `sessions_spawn` no longer fails with connection refused

## Why this approach over client-side fixes

An alternative approach (#29871) forces every internal `callGateway()` callsite to use loopback. That requires threading `forceLoopback` through multiple files and call paths. This PR fixes the problem at the source — the gateway simply binds to both addresses — which is simpler, less invasive, and requires no client-side changes.

target.title: feat(config): use datetime suffix for backup rotation instead of numeric
target.body:
Replace numeric backup rotation (`.bak.1`, `.bak.2`, …) with datetime-stamped suffixes (`.bak.YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` in UTC) so users can identify known-good backups at a glance without checking file modification times.

Closes #39923

## Summary

- **`rotateConfigBackups()`** — renames `.bak` → `.bak.YYYYMMDD-HHmmss` instead of shifting numbered slots
- **`cleanOrphanBackups()`** — keeps the N-1 most recent datetime-stamped backups (lexicographic sort = chronological); removes legacy numbered files (`.bak.1`, `.bak.2`) and other orphans on first upgrade run
- **`hardenBackupPermissions()`** — discovers datetime-stamped files via `readdir` instead of iterating a fixed numeric range
- **Collision handling** — appends `-2`, `-3`, … suffix for sub-second collisions
- **Backward compatible** — legacy `.bak.1`–`.bak.4` files are cleaned up automatically on first maintenance cycle

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / enhancement

## Test plan

- [x] All 8 tests pass (`vitest run src/config/config.backup-rotation.test.ts`) — 7 passed, 1 skipped (chmod on Windows)
- [x] New tests cover: datetime rotation, 5-deep ring enforcement, legacy numbered file cleanup, capacity limit pruning, collision suffix format validation, `isDatetimeSuffix()` unit tests
- [x] `maintainConfigBackups` compose test updated for datetime-stamped slots

target.title: fix(discord): remove invalid message_reference from webhook payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- Remove `message_reference` from Discord webhook execute payloads
- The [Execute Webhook](https://docs.discord.com/developers/resources/webhook) endpoint does not support this field; it is only valid on the [Create Message](https://docs.discord.com/developers/resources/message) endpoint (bot-token)
- The field was silently ignored by Discord, but sending unsupported fields risks future rejection

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify webhook sends still succeed without `message_reference`
- [ ] Confirm bot-token reply behavior (`replyTo` via `sendMessageDiscord`) is unaffected

target.title: fix(ui): prefer matching object variant in config coercion
target.body:
## Summary

Prefer the matching object variant when coercing config form values through `anyOf` / `oneOf` unions.

## Problem

`coerceFormValues()` previously recursed into the first object variant it encountered for object-valued unions. In schemas that distinguish object variants using `const` discriminator fields, this could coerce nested values using the wrong variant.

For example, a value like `{ kind: "named", retries: "03" }` could be coerced using a preceding `numeric` variant and incorrectly turn `retries` into `3`.

## Changes

- prefer object variants whose top-level property `const` constraints match the current value- fall back to the first object variant only when no discriminator match exists- add regression tests for both `anyOf` and `oneOf` object unions

## Notes

This keeps coercion behavior unchanged for string/number/boolean unions while making object-union coercion more accurate for discriminated schemas.

target.title: feat(feishu): expose non-bot @mentions in inbound conversation metadata
target.body:
## Problem

In a Feishu group chat with multiple bots (e.g. `龙虾小子` + `Arbiter`), when a user types `@Arbiter hello`, the `@Arbiter` is a Feishu rich-text mention element — **not plain text**. The receiving bot only sees the stripped message body (`hello`), with no signal that a *different* bot was addressed.

Result: both bots respond, even though only one was addressed.

## Root Cause

The Feishu plugin already has full mention data in `event.message.mentions`, and `normalizeMentions()` converts mention keys to `<at user_id="...">` tags in the message body. However, this normalized form still doesn't give the agent a clean, structured list of *who* was mentioned. The agent needs to pattern-match against raw HTML tags in an untrusted string — fragile and easy to miss.

## Fix

Surface the structured mention list directly in the **trusted inbound conversation-info metadata block** that agents already read.

**Three small changes:**

1. `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` — collect all non-self mentions from `event.message.mentions` and pass them as `Mentions` in the template context.
2. `src/auto-reply/templating.ts` — add `Mentions?: Array<{ id: string; name: string }>` to `TemplateContext`.
3. `src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.ts` — include `mentions` in the conversation-info user-role block when present.

## Result

When a user sends `@Arbiter hello` in a group, the receiving bot now sees:

```json
{
  "message_id": "...",
  "is_group_chat": true,
  "mentions": [{"id": "ou_arbiter_openid", "name": "Arbiter"}]
}
```

The agent can compare `mentions[*].name` (or `id`) against its known sibling bots and emit `NO_REPLY` immediately — no regex on message body required.

## Notes

- Only non-self mentions are included (the bot's own mention is already captured by `was_mentioned`).
- The field is omitted entirely when there are no non-self mentions (zero overhead for typical messages).
- No breaking changes; existing agents that don't use `mentions` are unaffected.

---

*Discovered while running two bots (龙虾小子 + Arbiter) in the same Feishu group. Both would respond to `@Arbiter` messages because the mention wasn't detectable from the agent's perspective.*

## Summary

Fixes two bugs in TUI session title derivation:

1. Bug 1: extractTextFromContent did not strip inbound metadata blocks before extracting the first user message, causing session titles to show untrusted metadata instead of actual message content.

target.title: fix(synology-chat,twitch,zalouser): clear timeout timers in Promise.race patterns
target.body:
## Summary

Several extensions use `Promise.race` with `setTimeout`-based timeouts but never clear the timer when the main promise wins the race. When the winning promise resolves/rejects first, the losing `setTimeout` fires later — either rejecting an unlistened promise (causing `unhandledRejection`) or keeping a timer handle alive unnecessarily.

**Affected extensions:**
- **synology-chat** `webhook-handler.ts`: 120s agent response timeout rejects after nobody is listening
- **twitch** `probe.ts`: connection timeout rejects after successful connect
- **zalouser** `probe.ts`: timeout timer keeps running after user info resolves

This is the same class of bug fixed in the Nostr extension (#39742) — systematic audit found these additional instances.

## Changes

Store timer handles and clear them in `finally` blocks so timers are always cleaned up regardless of which side wins the race.

## Test plan

- [x] `npx vitest run extensions/synology-chat/` — all tests pass
- [x] `npx vitest run extensions/twitch/` — all tests pass
- [x] `npx vitest run extensions/zalouser/` — all tests pass
- [x] 309 total tests, 0 failures
- [x] No functional behavior change — only timer cleanup added

target.title: fix: make isMemoryPath and isEvergreenMemoryPath case-insensitive for directory prefix
target.body:
## Problem

`isMemoryPath` in `src/memory/internal.ts` and `isEvergreenMemoryPath` in `src/memory/temporal-decay.ts` accept both `MEMORY.md` and `memory.md` as root memory files, but only check the lowercase `memory/` prefix for subdirectory paths.

On case-insensitive filesystems (macOS HFS+/APFS default), the memory directory can legitimately be named `MEMORY/`, `Memory/`, or any other case variant. Files under these directories would not be recognized as memory paths, causing them to:
1. Be excluded from memory indexing in `manager.ts`
2. Not receive temporal decay exemption (evergreen files would erroneously decay)

## Fix

- `isMemoryPath`: compare the lowercased normalized path against `memory/` prefix
- `isEvergreenMemoryPath`: same case-insensitive prefix check

## Tests

Added new `isMemoryPath` test suite covering:
- Root memory files (`MEMORY.md`, `memory.md`)
- Case-insensitive subdirectory matching (`MEMORY/notes.md`, `Memory/daily.md`)
- Non-memory path rejection

target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: fix(secrets): show irreversibility prompt for interactive confirm path (#83883)
target.body:
Fixes #83883.

`openclaw secrets configure` has two paths that reach the same `runSecretsApply` call:

1. **Non-interactive:** user passes `--apply`. `shouldApply` starts true. The one-way-migration confirmation runs if `!--yes && !--json`.
2. **Interactive:** user omits `--apply`, gets a "Apply this plan now?" prompt, answers yes. `shouldApply` becomes true via that prompt.

Today's gate at `src/cli/secrets-cli.ts:224` derives `needsIrreversiblePrompt = Boolean(opts.apply)`. That's `true` for path (1) and `false` for path (2). The interactive yes-path silently skips the one-way-migration warning and proceeds to a destructive apply — exactly the inverse of what an irreversibility prompt is for. The model of the user knowingly opting in via `--apply` is the weaker case; the interactive user who just answered "yes" is the one most likely to benefit from the warning.

## Changes

- `src/cli/secrets-cli.ts`: re-derive `needsIrreversiblePrompt` from `shouldApply && !opts.yes && !opts.json` inside the `if (shouldApply)` block. The warning now appears whenever apply is proceeding and the user hasn't opted out via `--yes` or `--json`, regardless of how `shouldApply` became true.

Diff stat: 1 file, +8 / -2.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Sanitized issue evidence — the `if (shouldApply) { … }` block at `secrets-cli.ts:223-235` had a `Boolean(opts.apply)` gate that excluded the interactive-yes path from seeing the warning. The new gate keys off `shouldApply` itself.

- **Real environment tested:** Local Node 22.x. Probe at `/tmp/probe_83883.mjs` does both halves of the proof. (a) Parses the patched `secrets-cli.ts` and verifies the new derivation `shouldApply && !opts.yes && !opts.json` is present and the old `Boolean(opts.apply)` derivation is gone. (b) Replays the gate logic against six (`opts`, `interactiveYes`) combinations — confirming the buggy shape skipped the irreversibility prompt on the interactive-yes path (the #83883 symptom), the patched shape shows it, and four no-regression cases for `--apply --yes`, `--apply` alone, `--json`, and interactive-no all behave the same as before.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node /tmp/probe_83883.mjs`

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
PASS: needsIrreversiblePrompt is now derived from shouldApply (not opts.apply)
PASS: old `Boolean(opts.apply)` gating is removed
PASS: replay (buggy): interactive yes → irreversible prompt SKIPPED — confirms #83883
PASS: replay (patched): interactive yes → irreversible prompt now SHOWN before apply
PASS: replay (patched, --apply --yes): no prompts — non-interactive yes flow unchanged
PASS: replay (patched, --apply alone): irreversible prompt shown — legacy --apply behavior preserved
PASS: replay (patched, --json): no prompts — JSON mode preserved
PASS: replay (patched, interactive no): apply skipped, no irreversible prompt

ALL CASES PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix:** An interactive `openclaw secrets configure` flow that ends with the user answering "yes" to "Apply this plan now?" now shows the one-way-migration confirmation immediately afterward, before any `runSecretsApply` invocation. The `--apply` / `--apply --yes` / `--json` paths are unchanged.

- **What was not tested:** Live secret-manager backends (1Password, AWS, Vault) — the fix is purely in the CLI prompt sequencing and does not touch any provider logic. The probe replays the exact gate logic at the call site without coupling to the broader secrets pipeline.

## Audit (per CLAUDE rules — all 5 steps)

- **Existing-helper check:** No predicate helper introduced — this is a one-line gate-condition rewrite from `Boolean(opts.apply)` to `shouldApply && !opts.yes && !opts.json`. PASS
- **Shared-helper caller check:** `needsIrreversiblePrompt` is a local within the `secrets configure` action handler; not exported or shared. PASS
- **Broader-fix rival scan:** `gh pr list --search '83883 in:title,body'` and `gh pr list --search 'irreversibility secrets configure'` return no open PRs. Issue timeline shows zero cross-references. PASS
- **Recent-merge audit:** `git log --oneline -5 -- src/cli/secrets-cli.ts` shows `e1061a8b46 test(live): tolerate provider drift in release checks` — unrelated to the configure-apply flow. PASS
- **Prototype-pollution scan:** N/A — pure boolean rewrite on locally-bound variables.

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

target.title: Feat/google genai sdk as common provider across Google AI Studio and Google Cloud
target.body:
## Summary

This PR integrates the new unified `@google/genai` SDK into OpenClaw, enabling support for both Google AI Studio (Gemini API) and Vertex AI (Google Cloud) via a single provider (`google-genai`).

- **Problem**: Google AI Studio `API_KEY` and Google Cloud Vertex AI are confusing to users, and hard to configure - non-API_KEY auth needs to be standardized in several spots in the codebase.
- **Why it matters**: IAM or ADC auth is more secure than API_KEYs and may help users get access.
- **What changed**: Similar to the AWS SDK provider, we added a Google Gen AI SDK which is unified across endpoints and maintained by Google.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: We did not yet deprecate or migrate other providers, but that's a reasonable next step.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #48033, #49191, #52648, #62212 (Resolves/improves Vertex AI ADC authentication issues by providing a working SDK integration).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [X] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/google-genai.live.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Successful streaming and authentication for both API Key and Vertex AI (ADC) paths.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the actual SDK network calls and authentication resolution against live Google endpoints.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None (this is a new provider integration).
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added a new provider `google-genai` that can be used in `openclaw.json`.
- Added support for `google-genai-sdk` authentication mode.
- Defaulted Vertex AI location to `"global"` to better support modern model aliases (like `gemini-flash-latest`).

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before (Vertex AI):
[User Config] -> [Legacy Auth Resolution] -> [Failures on ADC/IAM paths]

After (google-genai):
[User Config] -> [google-genai-sdk Auth] -> [Checks API Key -> Falls back to ADC (global default)] -> [Success]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: 
  - **Risk**: New network calls to Google GenAI endpoints.
  - **Mitigation**: The calls use standard official Google SDKs and follow secure Application Default Credentials (ADC) patterns, which are preferred over static API keys.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node.js (ESM)
- Model/provider: `gemini-flash-latest` via `google-genai`
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Set `GEMINI_LIVE_TEST=1`, `GEMINI_API_KEY` or `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`.

### Steps

1. Configure environment with either an API Key or a Service Account JSON file.
2. Run the live test: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/google-genai.live.test.ts`

### Expected

- Both tests (API Key and Vertex AI) pass and stream content successfully.

### Actual

- Both tests passed successfully after applying a workaround for a known `gaxios` bug in Node ESM.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [X] Failing test/log before + passing after (The Vertex AI test originally failed with a `gaxios` dynamic import error before the polyfill fix).
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Live streaming via API Key and live streaming via Vertex AI using a Service Account JSON key.
- Edge cases checked: Verified that omitting the location defaults to `global` and successfully resolves the `gemini-flash-latest` alias on Vertex AI.
- What you did **not** verify: Did not verify `gcloud auth application-default login` directly due to environment constraints on the test machine, but verified the identical ADC resolution path via a service account file.

## Review Conversations

- [X] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [X] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes` - adds new config options for the new provider).
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The underlying `gaxios` library has a bug in Node ESM environments where it fails to dynamically import `node-fetch` when checking for `window.fetch`.
  - Mitigation: We added documentation in `docs/google-genai-auth.md` explaining the workaround (`global.window = globalThis`) and applied it in the test file.

target.title: fix(tasks): silence acp child-session completion banners
target.body:
## What
- detect ACP child-session terminal tasks via `childSessionKey` instead of the nonexistent `spawnedBy` task field
- silently wake the parent session for successful non-blocked ACP child-session completions before notify-policy gating
- add regressions for same-child-session multi-run behavior, blocked visibility, non-ACP isolation, and silent-notify wake behavior

## Why
- repeated `Background task done` banners were being delivered per runId even when those runs belonged to the same ACP child session
- successful ACP child-session follow-up runs should resume the parent silently, while blocked and failure paths must remain visible

## Testing
- `pnpm test src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm lint -- src/tasks/task-executor-policy.ts src/tasks/task-registry.ts src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts`
- pre-commit hook `pnpm check` passed during commit
